content:
{
  "title": "Dynamic Adversarial Prompt Filtering: Enhancing LLM Robustness via Meta-Prompting",
  "problem": "Large language models are vulnerable to adversarial prompts and jailbreak attempts that bypass safety filters. Existing static prompting methods often fail to detect cleverly obfuscated or novel attack patterns.",
  "motivation": "Ensuring robust alignment of LLMs is critical as adversarial jailbreaks proliferate and models are widely deployed. There is a pressing need for prompting strategies that adapt dynamically to adversarial intent and generalize across attack types.",
  "method": "We propose Dynamic Adversarial Prompt Filtering (DAPF), a two-stage meta-prompting approach. First, an auxiliary meta-prompt instructs the LLM to rephrase and summarize the user input, explicitly flagging suspicious intent (e.g., requests for restricted information, obfuscated commands). The main prompt then conditions its response on this flagged summary, refusing or modifying outputs when the meta-prompt detects adversarial content. This process is implemented via in-context exemplars of flagged and safe summaries, without external classifiers or fine-tuning.",
  "experiment_plan": "Test DAPF on a curated set of adversarial and benign prompts from jailbreak benchmarks (e.g., JailbreakBench, AdvBench). Compare against standard prompting and chain-of-thought prompting as baselines. Measure attack success rate (ASR), false positive rate, and user utility preservation."
}
